sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has two white chambers separated by a purple vertical bar. The left chamber has a charcoal frame and a green frame with white center. Between chambers is a passage with a green frame (black center) and a white 3x3 square. The right chamber has a charcoal frame. The white 3x3 square at [30-32, 30-32] appears to be the player avatar in the passage area.
- Goal model: Likely need to navigate the player through the level, possibly collecting items or reaching the right chamber.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the white square avatar.
- Plan: Try moving the player (white square) to explore - first try moving right toward the purple wall to understand boundaries, then explore other directions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after RIGHT. Let me compare the before and after frames to see what moved. I need to check the segmentation to see which objects changed position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after to see what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

# Get node positions by hash
def get_node_by_hash(nodes, target_hash):
    for n in nodes:
        if n['hash'] == target_hash:
            return n
    return None

# Check the white square (node 8 hash) and green frames
white_sq_hash = 'eb5c9d9677b93af6'
green_frame_hash = 'df604ed27e7e83b0'

before_white = get_node_by_hash(before['nodes'], white_sq_hash)
after_white = get_node_by_hash(after['nodes'], white_sq_hash)

print("White square (3x3):")
print(f"  Before: boundary={before_white['boundary'] if before_white else 'None'}")
print(f"  After:  boundary={after_white['boundary'] if after_white else 'None'}")

# Check green frames
before_green = [n for n in before['nodes'] if n['hash'] == green_frame_hash]
after_green = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['hash'] == green_frame_hash]
print(f"\nGreen frames (before): {[(n['id'], n['boundary']) for n in before_green]}")
print(f"Green frames (after): {[(n['id'], n['boundary']) for n in after_green]}")

# Also check the white center pixels
before_centers = [n for n in before['nodes'] if n['pixels'] == 1]
after_centers = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['pixels'] == 1]
print(f"\nSingle pixels (before): {[(n['id'], n['color'], n['boundary']) for n in before_centers]}")
print(f"Single pixels (after): {[(n['id'], n['color'], n['boundary']) for n in after_centers]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see what changed:
1. The green frame (id 6) moved from [30,18]-[32,20] to [30,21]-[32,23] (shifted right by 3)
2. The white single pixel inside it moved from [31,19] to [31,22] (also shifted right by 3)
3. The other green frame (id 7/8) stayed at [30,27]-[32,29]
4. The black pixel at [31,28] stayed
So pressing RIGHT moves the green frame with the white center (the one in the left chamber) to the right by 3 cells. This is the player avatar - the green frame with white center.
The white 3x3 square at [27,45] didn't move - that's a static object in the right chamber.
Let me understand the layout better:
- Player: green frame with white center, starts at [30,18]-[32,20], center at [31,19]
- Target/obstacle: green frame with black center at [30,27]-[32,29]
- Purple wall at cols 33-37
- Right chamber has a charcoal frame
The goal is likely to get the player through the passage. Let me try moving more to understand the mechanics.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: The green frame with white center is the player avatar. RIGHT moved it 3 cells right (from col 18-20 to 21-23). The green frame with black center and the charcoal frames are static objects. The purple bar is a wall.
Goal model: Navigate the player through the passage to the right chamber.
Plan: Continue moving RIGHT to progress through the level. The passage seems to go between the green frame (black center) and the purple wall.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving RIGHT several more times to navigate through the passage
# Player center was at [31,19], moved to [31,22] after one RIGHT
# The green frame with black center is at [30,27]-[32,29]
# Need to get past it to reach the right chamber
# Let me try RIGHT again to see the movement pattern
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
